title: Holy Orders
html:
Big Question

Why do we need priests? Can't we go directly to God?

It was 1818. In a small village in France called Ars, about thirty kilometers north of Lyon, a young priest arrived. He was twenty-nine years old, spoke with difficulty due to a stutter, and had struggled enormously to complete his seminary studies — in fact, he nearly was sent home because he could not pass Latin. The neighbors looked at him with skepticism: «This man is going to be our pastor?»

His name was John Mary Vianney. Upon arriving at Ars, he knelt in the street and kissed the ground. He had no eloquence, no titles, no charisma according to worldly standards. But he had something that would transform not only that village but all of France: he spent hours in the confessional, sometimes sixteen and eighteen hours a day, hearing sins and giving absolution. Soon pilgrims began arriving from everywhere. Special railway stations were built. Twenty thousand pilgrims came each year. The village had two hundred inhabitants.

What did the Curé of Ars have? His secret was a certainty: «The priesthood is the love of the Heart of Jesus.» He did not preach about himself. He did not absolve in his own name. Every time a penitent entered the confessional, it was Christ himself who received him. The Holy Curé of Ars had understood what the Catechism teaches: the priest acts in persona Christi Capitis — in the person of Christ the Head. Not by his personal holiness, but because Christ himself acts through him.

This lesson addresses one of the most misunderstood sacraments of our faith. In an age that distrusts all authority and celebrates individualism, the idea of an ordained priesthood, with consecrated men who mediate between God and humanity, can seem outdated or unnecessary. But the Catechism reveals that Holy Orders is not a human invention: it is a gift of Christ to his Church, a prolongation of his one eternal priesthood, instituted at the Last Supper when he said: «Do this in memory of me» (Lk 22:19).

Activity

Imagine you are lost in a dark forest and you are afraid. Suddenly, someone appears with a flashlight and says: «Come, I know the way.» That is what priests are like. They are not the light; the light is Christ. But they carry the flashlight. They have been chosen and consecrated to guide us toward Jesus.

Draw two hands: one that gives and one that receives. In the hand that gives, write words a priest has spoken to you in confession, in a homily, or in counsel. In the hand that receives, write what you can offer for priests (your prayer, your support, your gratitude).

1. What Is Holy Orders?

The Catechism defines Holy Orders as «the sacrament through which the mission entrusted by Christ to his apostles continues to be exercised in the Church until the end of time» (CCC 1536). It is, therefore, the sacrament of apostolic ministry. While Baptism and Confirmation order the faithful for the common mission of every Christian, Holy Orders confers a special character: it configures the ordained person with Christ the Priest, Shepherd, and Head of the Church.

It comprises three degrees: episcopate (bishop), presbyterate (priest), and diaconate (deacon). All three are necessary for the organic life of the Church (CCC 1554). None exists in isolation; they form a hierarchical structure of service, not of worldly power. The term «hierarchy» comes from the Greek hierá arché: «sacred origin,» not «domination.»

Common Priesthood and Ministerial Priesthood

The Second Vatican Council, and the Catechism with it, clearly distinguishes two participations in the one priesthood of Christ (CCC 1546-1547):

  • The common priesthood of all the faithful: received in Baptism, it consists in offering one's own life as a spiritual sacrifice, proclaiming the Gospel by word and witness, and actively participating in the liturgy. Every baptized person is priest, prophet, and king.
  • The ministerial or hierarchical priesthood: conferred by the sacrament of Holy Orders, it is at the service of the common priesthood. The ordained minister is not «more Christian» than others, but he has received a sacred power to act in the name of Christ the Head.

The Catechism insists: these two priesthoods «are ordered one to another» and «each in its own proper way shares in the one priesthood of Christ» (CCC 1547). They do not compete; they complement each other. The ministerial priest does not replace the lay Christian; he serves him so that he may live his baptismal vocation fully. As Saint Augustine said: «For you I am a bishop; with you I am a Christian.»

The Three Degrees of Orders

Degree What It Is Primary Function
Bishop Successor of the apostles, receives the fullness of the sacrament of Holy Orders (CCC 1555-1556) Teach (magisterium), sanctify (liturgy, especially Confirmation and Holy Orders), govern (pastoral care of the diocese). He is the visible principle of unity in his particular Church.
Priest Co-worker of the bishop, configured to Christ the Priest (CCC 1562-1568) Preach the Gospel, celebrate the Eucharist and other sacraments (except Holy Orders in its fullness), pastorally guide the community. He acts in the person of Christ the Head.
Deacon Configured to Christ the Servant (CCC 1569-1571) Service of the Word (proclaim the Gospel, preach), service of the Liturgy (baptize, assist at marriage, preside at funerals), and service of charity (the poor, the sick, the marginalized).

Context

In the early Church, the apostles instituted the diaconate to take care of material needs so they would not neglect prayer and preaching (Acts 6:1-7). Saints Stephen and Philip were the first deacons. The diaconate fell into disuse as a permanent degree for centuries and was restored by the Second Vatican Council. Today, married men can be ordained as permanent deacons, and seminarians receive the diaconate as a step toward the priesthood.

You may wonder: if the three degrees are distinct, why do they form a single sacrament? Because all share in the one priesthood of Christ. The bishop possesses the fullness; the priest participates in a subordinated yet real way; the deacon is ordered not to priesthood but to ministry. Like a tree: the bishop is the trunk, the priests are the branches, the deacons are the roots sustaining from below.

2. Prefigurations in the Old Testament

God prepares his people progressively. The priesthood of the New Covenant does not emerge from nothing: it has deep roots in salvation history. The Catechism points out two great prefigurations (CCC 1539-1540, 1544-1545):

Melchizedek: The Priest-King

In Genesis 14, after Abram's victory over the enemy kings, a mysterious figure appears: Melchizedek, king of Salem (the future Jerusalem) and «priest of God Most High.» He offers bread and wine — a gesture the Fathers of the Church read as a figure of the Eucharist — and blesses Abram. Psalm 110 declares of the Messiah: «You are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek» (Ps 110:4). The Letter to the Hebrews develops this theme extensively: Jesus is the new and definitive Melchizedek, whose priesthood does not depend on human genealogy but on the power of an indestructible life (Heb 7:15-17).

The Levitical Priesthood

At Sinai, God institutes the priesthood of the tribe of Levi. Aaron and his sons are consecrated with oil to offer sacrifices, bless the people, and guard the sanctuary. This priesthood was hereditary, tied to the Mosaic Law, and offered animal sacrifices that had to be repeated continually.

But the Levitical priesthood was provisional, imperfect: the priests themselves were sinners and had to offer sacrifices first for their own sins. It pointed toward something greater: a priesthood not of blood, nor of law, but of grace; one that would offer not lambs but the Lamb of God; one that would not need repetition because it would be perfect and eternal.

Connection

Have you ever made an important promise? Perhaps to a friend, to your parents, to God. Human promises sometimes fail. The Old Testament priesthood was like that: priests who failed, sacrifices that had to be repeated, a system that never quite finished cleansing. But God was preparing something radically new: a priesthood based on an eternal promise, and a Priest who never fails. That Priest is Jesus.

Think: where in your life have you experienced the difference between human promises that fail and God's faithfulness that never fails?

3. Christ, the Supreme and Eternal High Priest

The Letter to the Hebrews is the great New Testament treatise on Christ's priesthood. In its central chapters, it develops a vision that transformed forever the understanding of priesthood:

«We do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who has been tested in every respect, just as we are, yet without sin. Let us therefore approach the throne of grace with boldness, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.»
(Heb 4:15-16)

Christ is the perfect High Priest for four reasons, which the Catechism gathers (CCC 1544-1545):

  1. He is of divine nature: he is not a mere man who offers to God; he is God himself who offers himself. The sacrificer and the victim are one.
  2. He is holy and innocent: he does not need to offer sacrifices for himself. His offering is purely for us.
  3. He offers a perfect sacrifice: not lambs or calves, but his own Body and his own Blood, offered once for all (Heb 7:27; 9:12).
  4. He lives forever to intercede: seated at the right hand of the Father, his priesthood never ends. There is no succession of Christs; there is one Christ who prolongs his priesthood through ordained ministers.

That is why the Christian priesthood is not parallel or alternative to Christ's: it is a sacramental participation in the one priesthood of Jesus Christ. There are not many priests; there is one Priest, Jesus Christ, who acts in and through ordained ministers.

Every high priest chosen from among mortals
is put in charge of things pertaining to God on their behalf,
to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins.
He is able to deal gently with the ignorant and wayward,
since he himself is subject to weakness.

— Hebrews 5:1-2

4. The Institution at the Last Supper

When did Jesus institute the priesthood? The Catechism answers clearly: at the Last Supper, when he instituted the Eucharist. Both sacraments are inseparable (CCC 1545, 1564).

Saint Luke narrates: «He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them, saying, "This is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me"» (Lk 22:19). The words «Do this in remembrance of me» are not a pious suggestion: they are a command. Jesus did not say «remember this»; he said «do this.» With these words, he commissioned the apostles — and their successors — to perpetuate the memorial of his Passover until his return.

In the Upper Room, during the Last Supper, Jesus performs three inseparable gestures:

  • He institutes the Eucharist: he transforms the bread and wine into his Body and Blood.
  • He institutes the ministerial priesthood: he gives the Twelve the power to sacramentally repeat this act. «The ministerial priesthood does not exist without the Eucharist; the Eucharist does not exist without the priesthood.»
  • He institutes the washing of feet: the priesthood is service. The Lord himself kneels before his disciples. The authority of the priest is authority to serve, not to dominate.

After the Resurrection, Jesus confirms this power with explicit words: «As the Father has sent me, so I send you. Receive the Holy Spirit. If you forgive the sins of any, they are forgiven them; if you retain the sins of any, they are retained» (Jn 20:21-23). The power to forgive sins is a constitutive part of ordained ministry and is intimately united to the power to consecrate the Eucharist.

Going Deeper

The relationship between Eucharist and priesthood was treated with magisterial clarity by John Paul II in his encyclical Ecclesia de Eucharistia (2003): «The Church draws her life from the Eucharist» (n. 1). And the Eucharist can only be celebrated by an ordained minister. This interdependence explains why the Church has always considered Holy Orders a sacrament of vocation, not of right.

Saint Thomas Aquinas dedicates extensive developments in the Summa Theologiae (STh III, q. 22 and Suppl. qq. 34-40) to this connection. At the moment of consecration, the priest does not speak in his own name but in Christ's name: he says «This is my body,» not «This is the body of Christ.» It is Christ who speaks and acts. That is why the Church insists that the priest must celebrate with reverence, faith, and humility: he is not performing tricks; he is lending his voice to God.

Recommended reading: Joseph Ratzinger, The Feast of Faith, chapter on the essence of the priesthood.

5. Acting in Persona Christi Capitis

The formula in persona Christi Capitis — in the person of Christ the Head — is central to understanding the ministerial priesthood (CCC 1548-1551). It means that when the priest celebrates the sacraments, he does not act by his own power or by delegation from the community, but by an ontological configuration with Christ.

This truth has enormous consequences:

  • The efficacy of the sacraments does not depend on the holiness of the minister. A priest in mortal sin, when consecrating, validly consecrates. It is not he who acts, but Christ. Saint Augustine fought the Donatist heresy precisely over this: the sacraments belong to Christ, not to the minister. The Church formulated it in the principle ex opere operato: the sacraments work by Christ's own action, not by the merits of minister or recipient.
  • The priest is instrument, not source. Like a paintbrush in a painter's hands. The brush does not paint; the artist paints. The priest does not save; Christ saves. But Christ has willed to need that brush.
  • The humility of ministry. If the priest truly understood that it is Christ who acts through him, he would never take pride. As the Curé of Ars said: «If we really understood what a priest is, we would die: not of fear, but of love.»

Apostolic Succession

The efficacy of the priesthood depends on the minister having been validly ordained within apostolic succession. This means that every bishop can trace his ordination back to the apostles themselves, through the uninterrupted laying on of hands. Apostolic succession is not institutional pride: it is the guarantee that the sacraments we celebrate are the very ones Christ instituted, not rites invented by each generation.

Saint Paul exhorts Timothy with words the Church reads at every ordination: «Do not neglect the gift that is in you, which was given to you through prophecy with the laying on of hands by the council of elders» (1 Tm 4:14). And in the second letter: «I remind you to rekindle the gift of God that is within you through the laying on of my hands» (2 Tm 1:6).

6. Priestly Celibacy: Total Gift of Self

In the Latin Church, priests and bishops are normally chosen from among celibate men who wish to keep celibacy for the Kingdom of Heaven (CCC 1579). In the Eastern Catholic Churches, the ordination of married men is permitted, though bishops are always celibate, and a married priest may not remarry should his wife die.

Celibacy is not a dogma of faith, but an ecclesiastical discipline that the Latin Church has considered supremely fitting for profoundly theological and pastoral reasons, not merely pragmatic ones.

The Catechism frames celibacy within a theology of gift:

«Bishops and priests who have freely accepted celibacy for the Kingdom of Heaven consecrate themselves to Christ with an undivided heart and give themselves more freely to his service.» (CCC 1579)

Celibacy is an eschatological sign: it anticipates the definitive state of heaven, where they neither marry nor are given in marriage (Mt 22:30). It is also a spousal sign: the priest is married to the Church, his mystical bride. Just as Christ gave himself totally for his Bride the Church, so the priest gives himself totally for the people of God. This language may sound strange to modern ears, but it is how the tradition has understood ministry: as a spiritual fatherhood that embraces all the faithful.

Not all priests live celibacy in the same way. Saint Josemaría Escrivá insisted that celibacy is not bachelorhood but «a gift of love.» He explained it thus: «The human heart, when it truly loves, is like a volcano. Celibacy does not extinguish that volcano: it channels it. Instead of loving one person, the priest loves everyone with the very love of Christ.»

Activity

Find the name of your pastor or the priest closest to your life. Write his name on a piece of paper. Then write three concrete things you can thank him for. Finally, commit to praying for him every day this week. Priests need our prayers more than we imagine.

7. The Mission of the Ordained Minister

The Catechism summarizes the priest's mission in the triple munus (office) of Christ (CCC 1581-1589):

To Teach (munus docendi)

The ordained minister is, by vocation, a preacher of the Gospel. He does not announce personal opinions or repeat cultural clichés; he proclaims the Word of God as the Church has received and understands it. The Sunday homily is one of the most important moments of his teaching ministry. That is why the Church asks him to prepare it in prayer and study: one does not improvise a speech; one preaches Christ.

To Sanctify (munus sanctificandi)

The priest is a steward of the mysteries of God (1 Cor 4:1), especially through the Eucharist and the sacrament of Reconciliation. The daily celebration of Mass is the center of his day. The confessional is his throne of mercy. The other sacraments — Baptism, Confirmation (by the bishop or delegate), Anointing, Marriage — are mediations of grace that pass through his hands.

To Govern (munus regendi)

The priest shepherds the community, guides the flock, makes decisions, resolves conflicts. His governance is not that of political power, but that of the Good Shepherd who knows his sheep, calls them by name, and lays down his life for them (Jn 10:11-15). Authority in the Church is always service and is measured by love, not by imposition.

8. Connection with Scripture

The New Testament is permeated by this priestly reality. Besides the texts already cited, special attention should be given to:

Jesus came and said to them:
«All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.
Go therefore and make disciples of all nations,
baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,
and teaching them to obey everything that I have commanded you.
And remember, I am with you always,
to the end of the age.»

— Matthew 28:18-20

The great missionary commission entrusted to the apostles includes teaching, baptizing, and making disciples. It is the seed of what will be the ordained ministry. And the final promise — «I am with you always» — is the assurance that Christ never abandons his Church or his ministers.

Saint Paul develops in Ephesians 4:11-13 the variety of ministries, all oriented toward building up the Body of Christ: «He himself gave some to be apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, some pastors and teachers, to equip the saints for the work of ministry.» Ordained ministry exists so that the whole Body may grow in holiness.

The YouCat, faithful to the structure of the Catechism, dedicates questions 248-259 to Holy Orders. Its question 248 opens: «What is the sacrament of Holy Orders?» And question 249 adds: «Why is priestly ordination denied to women?», answering with clarity: Christ chose men as apostles and the Church feels bound by that choice. It is not a matter of capability but of fidelity to the sacrament as Christ willed it.

For Reflection

1. Who is the first priest that comes to mind when you think of the ordained ministry? What impact has he had on your life?

Take three minutes to remember and give thanks for that specific priest. If he is still alive, consider writing him a short note of gratitude.

2. The Catechism teaches that there are two participations in the one priesthood of Christ: the common priesthood of all the baptized and the ministerial priesthood. How do you live your baptismal priesthood in daily life?

Think about your work, your family, your relationships: do you offer your life as a spiritual sacrifice? Do you bear witness to the Gospel?

3. «The priesthood is the love of the Heart of Jesus,» the Curé of Ars used to say. Have you seen that love reflected in any priest? What impressed you most?

Sometimes we see it in the patience of a confessor, in the joy of a pastor with children, in a priest who visits the sick without fanfare.

4. Priestly celibacy is a sign of total love for God and the Church. What do you think or feel when you hear celibacy spoken of? Does it strike you as valuable, hard to understand, admirable?

There are no wrong answers here. The Church invites you to understand it as a gift and a mystery, not as a dry obligation.

5. If Jesus asked you today, as he did the Twelve in the Upper Room: «Do you want to follow me?», what would you answer? Have you ever considered that God might be calling you to the priesthood or the consecrated life?

Don't dismiss the question too quickly. God calls some to priesthood, others to marriage, others to the consecrated or lay life. All are vocations to love. Which one is yours?

Key Takeaway

Holy Orders is neither a human invention nor a privilege: it is the gift by which Christ, the Supreme and Eternal High Priest, prolongs his saving presence in history through fragile men who — configured with him — preach, sanctify, and shepherd his people. The priest does not replace Christ; he is his instrument. And every time a priest says: «I absolve you» or «This is my Body,» it is Christ himself who speaks.

Cultural Resources

Patron Saint Saint John Mary Vianney (1786-1859), the Holy Curé of Ars, patron of all parish priests. His life is one of the most extraordinary biographies in Catholicism. He entered the seminary barely knowing how to read; he was ordained almost out of pity, because no one thought he could be a good priest; he ended up as confessor to all of France. The French film Le Curé d'Ars and the book The Curé of Ars by Francis Trochu are excellent introductions. Saint John XXIII dedicated his encyclical Sacerdotii Nostri Primordia (1959) to him, a beautiful meditation on the priesthood.

Witness Saint Josemaría Escrivá (1902-1975), founder of Opus Dei, lived his priesthood with an intensity that galvanized thousands of laypeople. His book Loving the Church contains a homily titled «A Priest Forever» that is a hymn of love to ordained ministry. For him, the priest was «a man who has let himself be taken by God, who has placed his whole life at the service of souls.» Available free at en.escrivaworks.org.

Communication Venerable Fulton J. Sheen (1895-1979) was the first great Catholic evangelist of television. His program Life Is Worth Living had thirty million weekly viewers. Sheen offered an Hour of Adoration before the Blessed Sacrament each day before preaching. His book The Priest Is Not His Own develops the spirituality of the priesthood as a holocaust of love: the priest is a victim who is consumed so that others may have life. Hundreds of his restored catecheses are available on YouTube.

Confessor Saint Pius of Pietrelcina (1887-1968), Padre Pio, spent up to eighteen hours in the confessional just like the Curé of Ars. One day he was asked how he could endure it. He replied: «The hunger of souls is stronger than the hunger of the body.» Padre Pio lived the mystery of the priesthood with the stigmata of the Passion on his body for fifty years. He was canonized by John Paul II in 2002. The film Padre Pio (Abel Ferrara, 2022) offers a cinematic approach to the early years of his religious life.

Indigenous Mission Saint Juan Diego (1474-1548) and indigenous priesthood. Our Lady of Guadalupe chose an indigenous man, not a Spaniard, as her messenger to Bishop Juan de Zumárraga. This was revolutionary in the 16th century and has profound resonance for the priesthood: the Church became incarnate in America through the witness of the humblest. Though Juan Diego was not an ordained priest, his mission as mediator between heaven and earth — carrying the roses and the image to the bishop — is a figure of priestly ministry as a bridge. Today, the Church in Mexico counts seminarians from indigenous communities who carry the Gospel in their native languages.

Art Francisco de Zurbarán (1598-1664), the great painter of the Spanish Baroque, portrayed the dignity of the priesthood like no other. His painting Saint Dominic of Guzmán shows the saint with a book and a dog with a torch, as light that illuminates. His series of Carthusian monks and his Apostolados capture the serenity and inner strength that flow from a consecrated life. His Crucified Christ (1627) is one of the most overwhelming images of the Priest who offers himself.

Youth Catechism YouCat (Youth Catechism), questions 248-259. It explains accessibly all aspects of Holy Orders: the three degrees, apostolic succession, celibacy, why only men, and how a priest lives his ministry. Each question includes a biblical citation, an icon, and references to the full Catechism. Available at youcat.org.

Audiovisual Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true) dedicates an episode to the sacrament of Holy Orders. Also the channel Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz has very accessible videos on the priesthood. In Spanish, Mater Mundi TV offers catechesis with doctrinal depth on this sacrament.

Music Hakuna Group Music, in their album Sencillamente, has the song «Tuyo soy» («I Am Yours») that many seminarians sing as an expression of their total self-giving. Also the Gregorian chant «Tu es Sacerdos in aeternum» — You are a priest forever — (Ps 110:4), sung at ordinations, is one of the oldest and most beautiful melodies of the Catholic liturgy.
